title: Corrected NPU FP16 compute nm64 boundary
hypothesis: The corrected nm32 dynamic-dispatch FP16 NPU point still uses only about 37% of the 1.5mm x 1.5mm macro core area, so nm64 should be measured before assuming a scheduler or memory hierarchy change is required.

direct_comparison.primary_question: Can the corrected current dynamic-dispatch NPU compute block close at nm64 in the same cmp33 physical envelope, and which flat or hierarchical mode is viable?
include: full npu_top wrapper with the corrected GEMM/VEC writeback path
flat_nomacro and hier_macro mode comparison using the existing cmp33 floorplan
boundary evidence if timing, placement, routing, or flow runtime fails
exclude: new static scheduler or NoC implementation
larger die or retuned memory hierarchy
quality-changing quantization or KV sharing assumptions

expected_benefit: sets the next physical compute-capacity anchor beyond corrected nm32
shows whether the current dynamic dispatcher remains a usable scaling baseline
gives the system-level Llama7B model a better measured bound for compute parallelism before memory/NoC tradeoff sweeps
risks: nm64 may be expensive in OpenROAD and should be recorded as boundary evidence rather than retried indefinitely
the inherited cmp33 floorplan may become the limiting factor before the compute array itself
a single seed is enough for boundary scouting but not final stability characterization

required_evaluations.objective: Measure corrected NPU FP16 nm64 dynamic-dispatch boundary with cmp33 flat/hier mode comparison after the corrected nm1 through nm32 frontier.

expected_result.reason: Use nm64 timing, area, power, utilization, and flow status to decide whether to continue current dynamic dispatch scaling or switch to hierarchical/static scheduling.
